base_expression.text: tool wear
base_expression.label: failure symptom

enriched_expressions.definition: Tool wear is the gradual failure of cutting tools in machining, manifested as erosion or damage to the tool’s flank, rake face, or cutting edge. It includes types such as flank wear, crater wear, notch wear, and built‑up edge, and is influenced by operational parameters like spindle speed, feed rate, and cutting depth.
ontology_hints: Tool wear is a failure symptom and a degradation event affecting spatial objects (cutting tools) in a manufacturing environment.
It is a temporal process (TemporalEntity) that can be monitored or acted upon by operators.
Tool wear can be caused by high feed rates, low spindle speeds, or excessive cutting depth, and may trigger shutdowns or maintenance interventions.
Relevant properties: TRIGGERS (e.g., tool failure), CAUSES (operational parameters), REQUIRES (maintenance action), HANDLED_BY (operator), AFFECTS (cutting tool, machining quality).

evidence.content:
In machining, tool wear is the gradual failure of cutting tools due to regular operation. Tools affected include tipped tools, tool bits, and drill bits that are used with machine tools.
Types of wear include:

flank wear in which the portion of the tool in contact with the finished part erodes.  Can be described using the Tool Life Expectancy equation.
crater wear in which contact with chips erodes the rake face. This is somewhat normal for tool wear, and does not seriously degrade the use of a tool until it becomes serious enough to cause a cutting edge failure. Can be caused by spindle speed that is too low or a feed rate that is too high. In orthogonal cutting this typically occurs where the tool temperature is highest. Crater wear occurs approximately at a height equalling the cutting depth of the material. Crater wear depth (t0) = cutting depth
Notch wear which happens on both the insert rake and flank face along the depth of cut line causing localised damage to it primarily due to pressure welding of the chips. The chips literally get welded to the insert.
built-up edge in which material being machined builds up on the cutting edge. Some materials (notably aluminium and copp

evidence.content: {"aliases": ["maintenance timer expiration detection", "maintenance counter expiration detection"], "synonyms": ["maintenance timer expiration detection", "maintenance counter expiration detection"], "lexical_variants": ["maintenance timer elapsed", "maintenance counter expired", "maintenance due time reached"], "definition": "An event in which a monitoring system detects that one or more maintenance timers (counters) have reached their elapsed time, indicating that maintenance is due or overdue.", "ontology_hints": ["triggers MaintenanceAlarm", "causes MaintenanceDueEvent", "requires MaintenanceIntervention", "handled_by Technician", "part_of ManufacturingProcess", "affects ProductionLine", "observed_by MaintenanceSystem"]}
